content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 19, 2006

Ourania M. Vlahos
Attorney
Public Utilities Commission of
the State of California
505 Van Ness Avenue
San Francisco, CA 94102-3298

RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-06-011

Dear Ms. Vlahos:

This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Ms. Georgetta Gregory, who
will be employed as a supervisor in the Rail Operations Safety Branch of the Public
Utilities Commission (the "PUC"), for informal assistance' regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").2

QUESTION

Is Ms. Gregory, as a supervisor in the PUC's Rail Operations Safety Branch,
prohibited by the Act from performing certain duties affecting railroad companies
because her husband is employed by a railroad company that is regulated by the PUC?

CONCLUSION

As a public official employed by the PUC, Ms. Gregory may not make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use her official position to influence a
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on her husband's employer, Union Pacific Railroad Company

1 Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)

2 Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All statutory references herein are to the Government
Code unless otherwise indicated. All regulatory references herein are to Title 2 of the California Code of
Regulations unless otherwise indicated.

File No.I-06-011
Page No. 2

("UPRR") or UPRR's parent company, Union Pacific Corporation (which owns 100% of
UPRR's stock). Because Ms. Gregory has a community property interest in her
husband's income, she is deemed to have an economic interest in UPRR as well as
UPRR's parent company. She must analyze her duties to determine if they involve a
governmental decision affecting UPRR or UPRR's parent company on a case-by-case
basis.

FACTS

The PUC would like to employ Ms. Gregory as a supervisor ("OSS Supervisor")
in the Rail Operations Safety Branch of the Operations and Safety Section. This branch
is part of the PUC's Consumer Protection and Safety Division. The PUC regulates
railroad companies operating in California. As OSS Supervisor, Ms. Gregory would
work on matters concerning the safety of the operations of railroad companies in
California. Ms. Gregory's husband is an employee of UPRR, a railroad regulated by the
PUC. UPRR is one of only two long line railroad companies in California. It is a wholly
owned subsidiary of Union Pacific Corporation.

The OSS Supervisor oversees the work of inspectors in the Rail Operations Safety
Branch who inspect railroad companies operating in California to determine whether they
are complying with federal safety regulations. The Federal Railroad Administration
("FRA") promulgates regulations and imposes penalties for their violation. The FRA
imposes these penalties by relying on the work of PUC inspectors. In addition, inspectors
check railroad companies' compliance with PUC safety regulations, investigate railroad
accidents in California, and respond to safety-related inquiries or complaints from other
agencies, organizations and the public.

Not only is UPRR a wholly owned subsidiary of Union Pacific Corporation, it is
its single line of business and its principle source of revenue and operating income. (You
state that for fiscal year 2004, of Union Pacific Corporation's $12.2 of revenue $11.7
billion came from UPRR).

You state that Ms. Gregory has an economic interest in UPRR. You have
described numerous instances in which you ask whether UPRR will be directly or
indirectly involved in a governmental decision:

1. Discussions with FRA staff regarding coordination of investigative
activities of the FRA and the PUC as to railroad companies generally and not as to a
particular company.

2. Making or participating in making decisions to conduct safety inspections of
UPRR or to investigate an accident, inquiry or complaint.

3. Participating in informal discussions with UPRR after it is found to be out of
compliance to try to achieve compliance.

File No.1-06-011
Page No. 3

3a. Informal discussions with UPRR (at the direction and under the supervision of
the FRA) after UPRR has been found to be out of compliance to try to achieve
compliance.

4a. Asking the PUC to open a formal investigation.

4b. Asking the FRA to assess penalties against UPRR.

5. Participating in informal discussions with management of UPRR concerning
procedures and practices in general.

6. Working on matters relating to adoption of safety rules by the FRA.

7a, 7b and 7c. Working on rules or policies to be considered for adoption by the
PUC.

8 and 8a. Advising PUC or staff and testifying before the legislature on
legislative matters addressing railroad safety.

9. Working on litigation in which UPRR is not currently a party but where a third
party has challenged a PUC decision in a previous matter in which UPRR had been a
party.

You have also asked what materiality standard should be applied to UPRR.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 of the Act provides that "[n]o public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has a financial interest." Regulations 18700 through 18709 set forth an eight-
step analysis in determining whether a public official has a conflict of interest in a
governmental decision he or she will be making.

Step One. As the OSS Supervisor, is Ms. Gregory a "public official" within
the meaning of section 87100?

Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." As an employee of the PUC, which is
a state government agency, the OSS Supervisor is a public official.

Step Two. Will Ms. Gregory be making, participating in making or
influencing a governmental decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or

File No.I-06-011
Page No. 4

commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) A public official
"participates in a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the governmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)

Regulation 18702.2(b) is particularly broad in its reach, often making it difficult
to determine without detailed facts whether an official is participating in making a
governmental decision.

"A public official 'participates in making a governmental
decision'…when, acting within the authority of his or her
position, the official…advises or makes recommendations to
the decisionmaker either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review, by (1) conducting research
or making any investigation which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official and the purpose of which
is to influence a governmental decision…or (2) preparing or
presenting any report, analysis, or opinion, orally or in
writing, which requires the exercise of judgment on the part
of the official and the purpose of which is to influence a
governmental decision referenced in Title 2, California
Code of Regulations, section 18701(a)(2)(A)." (Regulation
18702.2(b).)

Accordingly, Ms. Gregory will be participating in making a governmental
decision unless there is an intervening "significant substantive review" of her work
before a decision is made.

Ms. Gregory is responsible for determining, based upon all relevant facts, whether
the decision or activity involves a governmental decision.

Step Three. What are Ms. Gregory's economic interests? What are the
possible sources of a financial conflict of interest?

The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are described in
section 87103 and regulations 18703-18703.5. There are six kinds of economic interests:

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section
87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a).)

File No.I-06-011
Page No. 5

• A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (Section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b).)

• A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or
she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b);
regulation 18703.2.)

• An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); regulation18703.3.)

• A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or
her if the gifts total $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87103(e); regulation18703.4.)

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate
family. This is commonly referred to as the "personal financial effects"
rule. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.)

Ms. Gregory has at least three relevant economic interests under your facts.

First, Ms. Gregory has an economic interest in UPRR. A public official's
"income" for conflict-of-interest purposes includes his or her community property
interest in the income of his or her spouse. (Section 82030(a).) Accordingly, if Ms.
Gregory's spouse has received $1,000 or more from UPRR within 12 months prior to
making a relevant governmental decision, UPRR will be a source of income to her.³

Second, Ms. Gregory has an economic interest in Union Pacific Corporation.
Regulation 18703.3(a)(2) provides that "an official has an economic interest in a business
entity which is a parent or subsidiary of, or is otherwise related to, a business entity in
which the official has an interest as defined in Government Code Section 87103(c)."
Regulation 18703.1 states that "a parent-subsidiary relationship exists when one
corporation directly or indirectly owns shares possessing more than 50 percent of the
voting power of any other corporation." Because Union Pacific Corporation owns all the
shares of UPRR, Ms. Gregory has an economic interest in Union Pacific Corporation.

Third, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family.
Section 82029 defines "immediate family" to include an official's spouse. Hence,
Ms. Gregory has an economic interest not only in her own personal finances but those of
her spouse.

³ The payment to her spouse would be $1,000 in order for Ms. Gregory's community property
share to reach the $500 threshold of section 87103(c).

File No.I-06-011
Page No. 6

**Step Four. Will Ms. Gregory's economic interests be directly or indirectly
involved in a governmental decision?**

Regulation 18704.1(a) provides that a person (including business entities and
sources of income) "is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when
that person, either directly or by an agent: (1) initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request, or (2) is a
named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person."

Each governmental decision must be analyzed in terms of whether UPRR, Union
Pacific Corporation and the personal finances of Ms. Gregory or her spouse will be
directly or indirectly involved.

**UPRR - Your questions 2, 3, 3a, 4a and 4b all involve UPRR as the subject of a
proceeding in which a governmental decision will be made. In question 2, UPRR would
be the subject of the decision whether to conduct an inspection or investigation. In
questions 3 and 3a, UPRR would be negotiating regarding its violation of a safety rule
and a potential enforcement action against it. In question 4a, UPRR would be the subject
of the decision by the PUC whether to open an investigation of UPPR. In question 4b,
UPRR would be the subject of a decision to assess penalties.**

Business entities and sources of income which are not directly involved in
governmental decisions under the rules quoted above are regarded as indirectly
involved. (Regulation 18704.1(b).)⁴

Generally, UPRR would be indirectly involved when engaging in general
discussions with FRA to coordinate investigative activities, (your question 1). As to
working on rules or policies for adoption by the PUC, (your questions 7a, 7b and 7c) and
advising the PUC or testifying before the legislature (your questions 8 and 8a),⁵ UPRR
may or may not be directly involved. We lack sufficient facts to make that
determination.

⁴ You state that UPRR is the only reportable business segment of Union Pacific Corporation and
that UPRR is its single line of business and its principal source of revenue. These facts, however, have no
relevance to the test set out in Regulation 18704.1.

⁵ Under certain circumstances, UPRR might be directly involved in these decisions if, for
example, UPRR currently operates under an entitlement which may be lost or altered by changes in a rule,
policy or legislation. You have advised that UPRR is one of only two long line railroad companies in
California. A proceeding that may appear general in its application may nevertheless have a specific
impact on UPRR's entitlement, in which case UPRR would be directly involved in the decision. (See
Fallon Advice Letter, No. A-85-050, City of Agoura Hills v. Local Agency Formation Commission of
County of Los Angeles (1988) 198 Cal.App.3d 480 and Gallien Advice Letter, No. A-99-092).

File No.I-06-011
Page No. 7

UPRR's involvement in the litigation described in question 9 also may or may not
be direct. We lack sufficient facts regarding the effect on UPRR of the outcome of the
litigation you generally describe to make that determination.

Union Pacific Corporation - Union Pacific Corporation may or may not be
directly involved in the governmental decisions you have identified depending on
whether, based upon all the facts, it will be the subject of any of the proceedings you
have described.

Personal Finances - Regulation 18704.5 provides that "a public official or his or
her immediate family is deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision which
has any financial effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate
family." Therefore, if any governmental decision you have identified has any financial
effect on the personal finances of Ms. Gregory or her husband, they are deemed to be
directly involved in the decision.

Step Five. Applicable Materiality Standard

Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify
the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest. Regulation 18705.3 –
18705.5 sets forth the materiality standards to be applied in different situations.

Directly Involved - For economic interests in business entities directly involved
in a decision, including business entities which are a source of income to an official, the
materiality standard is set forth in regulation 18705.1(b) which states: "[t]he financial
effects of a government decision on a business entity which is directly involved in
the governmental decision is presumed to be material."⁶

UPRR and United Pacific Corporation - Applying regulation 18705.1(b), the
financial effect of any governmental decision in which UPRR or United Pacific
Corporation is directly involved is presumed to be material.

Personal Finances - A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public
official's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period.
(Regulation 18705.5.)

Indirectly Involved - For economic interests in business entities indirectly
involved in a decision, including business entities which are sources of income to an
official, the materiality standards are set forth in regulation 18705.1(c). The standards
vary according to whether the business entity is a Fortune 500 company, is listed on the
New York Stock Exchange, American Stock Exchange, or NASDAQ, or according to the
size of the business entity measured by its pre-tax earnings.

⁶ This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any financial effect on the business entity. (Regulation 18705.1(b)(1).)

File No.J-06-011
Page No. 8

UPRR - We know that UPRR is not listed and so we focus on its earnings.
Regulation 18705.1(c)(2) applies to business entities whose most recent fiscal year pre-
tax earnings were $2.5 million or more. You have indicated that UPRR's pre-tax income
far exceeds $2.5 million. Accordingly, a governmental decision will have a material
effect upon UPRR if the decision results in an increase or decrease to UPRR's gross
revenues of $500,000 in a fiscal year; or would result in UPRR incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $200,000 or more; or would result in an increase or decrease in the value of
UPRR's assets or liabilities of $500,000 or more.

Union Pacific Corporation - You state that Union Pacific Corporation is a Fortune
500 company. Hence, if Union Pacific Corporation is indirectly involved in a
governmental decision, the financial effects of the decision on Union Pacific Corporation
is material if it is reasonably foreseeable the decision will result in an increase or
decrease in its gross revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000,000 or more; or will result in
Union Pacific Corporation incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500,000 or more; or
will result in an increase or decrease of the value of its assets or liabilities of $10,000,000
or more. (Regulation 18705.1(c)(1).)

Step Six. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a
governmental decision on Ms. Gregory's economic interests will meet the applicable
materiality standard? (Does a conflict-of-interest exist?)

"A public official has a conflict of interest if the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his/her economic interests..."
(Regulation 18700.) Once an official has determined the materiality standards applicable
to each of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is
"reasonably foreseeable" that the standard will be met.

A material financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if
it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met as a
result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be
certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Generally, each governmental decision is analyzed independently to determine if
there will be a foreseeable material financial effect on an official's financial interest. (In
re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops. 77.) Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is
foreseeable at the time a decision is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to
each case. (In re Thorner, supra.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of
fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), the foreseeability of a
particular financial effect is a determination that must be left, in most instances, to the
informed judgment of the public official.

File No.I-06-011
Page No. 9

Steps Seven & Eight. Does the "public generally" exception or the "legally required participation" exception apply?

Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the public official's economic interest is indistinguishable from the effect on the public generally (section 87103; regulations 18700(b)(7) and 18707(a)) or the official is legally required to participate (section 87103; regulation 18708). You have provided no facts indicating that either of these exceptions applies in this instance.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Is Ms. Gregory, as a supervisor in the PUC's Rail Operations Safety Branch,
prohibited by the Act from performing certain duties affecting railroad companies
because her husband is employed by a railroad company that is regulated by the PUC?

govemmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on herhusband's employer, Union Pacific Railroad Company

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 of the Act provides that "[n]o public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his
official position to influence a govemmental decision in which he knows or has reason to
know he has afrnancial interest." Regulations 18700 through 18709 set forth an eight-
step analysis in determining whether a public official has a conflict of interest in a
governmental decision he or she will be making.
Step One. As the OSS Supervisor, is Ms. Gregory a "public official,, within
the meaning of section 87100?
Section 82048 defines a public official as "every member, officer, employee or
consultant of a state or local government agency." As an employee of the PUC, which is
a state government agency, the OSS Supervisor is apublic official.
step Two. will Ms. Gregory be making, participating in making or
influencing a governmental decision?
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to arry course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.7.) A public official
"particrpates in a govemmental decision" when, actingwithin the authority of his or her
position and without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates,
advises, or makes recommendations to the decisionmaker regarding the govemmental
decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) A public official is attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a goveflrmental decision if for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official contacts or appears before any member, officer, employee, or
consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.3.)
Regulation I8702.2(b) is particularlybroad in its reach, often making it difficult
to determine without detailed facts whether an official is participating in making a
govemmental decision.
"A public official 'participates in making a governmental
decision'. ..when, acting within the authority of his or her
position, the official...advises or makes recofilmendations to
the decisionmaker either directly or without significant
intervening substantive review, bV (1) conducting research
or making any investigation which requires the exercise of
judgment on the part of the official and the pu{pose of which
is to influence a govemmental decision...or (2) preparing or
presenting any report, analysis, or opinion, orally or in
writing, which requires the exercise ofjudgment on the part
of the official and the purpose of which is to influence a
governmental decision referenced in Title 2, Califomia
Code of Regulations, section I 870 I (a) (2)(A) )' (Regulation
18702.2(b).)
Accordingly, Ms. Gregory will be participating in making a governmental
decision unless there is an intervening "significant substantive review" of her work
before a decision is made.
Ms. Gregory is responsible for determining, based upon all relevant facts, whether
the decision or activity involves a govemmental decision.
step Three. what are Ms. Gregory's economic interests? what are the
possible sources of a linancial conflict of interest?
The economic interests from which conflicts of interest may arise are described in
section 87103 and regulations 18703-18703.5. There are six kinds of economic interests:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more. (Section
871 03(a); regulation 1 8703. 1 (a).)

A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he
or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management. (Section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b).)
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or
she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more. (Section g7103(b);
regulation 18703.2.)
An official has an economic interest in any source of income, including
promised income, totaling $500 or more within 12 months prior to the
decision. (Section 87103(c); regulationl 8703.3.)
A public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or
her if the gifts total $360 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.
(Section 87 1 03(e); regulationl 87 03.4.)
A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses,
income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate
family. This is commonly referred to as the o'personal financial effects,,
rule. (Section 87103; regulation 18703.5.)
Ms. Gregory has at least three relevant economic interests under your facts.
First, Ms. Gregory has an economic interest in UpRR. A public official,s
"income" for conflict-of-interest purposes includes his or her community property
interest in the income of his or her spouse. (Section 82030(a).) Accordingly, if Ms.
Gregory's spouse has received $1,000 or more from UPRR within 12 months prior to
making a relevant governmental decision, UPRR will be a source of income to her.3
Second, Ms. Gregory has an economic interest in Union Pacific Corporation.
Regulation 18703.3(a)(2) provides that "an official has an economic interest in a business
entity which is a parent or subsidiary of or is otherwise related to, a business entity in
which the official has an interest as defined in Govemment Code Section 87103(cj.,,
Regulation 18703.1 states that"aparent-subsidiaryrelationship exists when one
corporation directly or indirectly owns shares possessing more than 50 percent of the
voting power of another corporation." Because Union Pacific Corporation owns all the
shares of UPRR, Ms. Gregory has an economic interest in Union Pacific Corporation.
Third, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal
expenses, income, assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family.
Section 82029 defines "immediate family" to include an official's spouse. Hence,
Ms. Gregory has an economic interest not only in her own personal finances but those of
her spouse.
3 The payment to her spouse would be $1,000 in order for Ms. Gregory's communityproperfy
share to reach the $500 threshold ofsection 87103(c).
Step Four. Will Ms. Gregory's economic interests be directly or indirectly
involved in a governmental decision?
Regulation 18704.1(a) provides that aperson (including business entities and
sources of income) "is directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when
that person, either directly or by an agent: (1) initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request, or (2) is a
named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any liiense, permit, or
other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.,,
Each governmental decision must be analyzedin terms of whether UpRR, Union
Pacific Corporation and the personal finances of Ms. Gregory or her spouse will be
directly or indirectly involved.
UPRR - Your questions 2,3,3a, 4a and,4b all involve UPRR as the subject of a
proceeding in which a governmental decision will be made. In question 2, UpRR would
be the subject of the decision whether to conduct an inspection oi investigation. ln
questions 3 and 3a, UPRR would be negotiating regarding its violation oia safety rule
and apotential enforcement action against it. In question 4a,UPRR would be thl subject
of the decision by the PUC whether to open an investigation of UPPR. In question 4b,
UPPR would be the subject of a decision to assess penalties.
Business entities and sources of income which are not directly involved in
governmental decisions under the rules quoted above are regarded as indirectly
involved. (Regulation 1 8704. 1 (b).)a
Generally, UPPR would be indirectly involved when engaging in general
discussions with FRA to coordinate investigative activities, (your q,r"stlor, 1). As to
working on rules or policies for adoption by the PUC, (your questions 7a, Tb and 7c) and
advising the PUC or testiffing before the legislature (your questions 8 and aa;,s Up'RR
may or may not be directly involved. We lack sufficient facts to make that
deterrnination.
o Yo,, state th1! UPRR is the only reportable business segment of Union Pacific Corporation and
that UPRR is its single line of business and its principal source of revenue. These facts, howwer, have no
relevance to the test set out in Regulation 18704.1.
t Undet certain circumstances, UPRR might be directly involved in these decisions if, for
example, UPRR currently operates under an entitlement which may be lost or altered by changes in a rule,
policy or legislation. You have advised that UPRR is one of only two long line railroai comp-anies in
California. A proceeding that may appear general in its application may nivertheless have a specific
impact on UPPR's entitlement, in which case UPRR would be directly involved in the decision. (See
Fallon Advice Letter, N9,,-4..85;050, City of Agoura Hills v. Local Agency Formation Commission of
County of Los Angeles (1988) 198 Cal.App.3d 480 and Gallien AdviJe Letter,No. 4-99-092).
UPRR's involvement in the litigation described in question 9 also may or may not
be direct. We lack sufficient facts regarding the effect on UPPR of the outcome of the
litigation you generally describe to make that determination.
Union Pacific Corporation - Union Pacific Corporation may or may not be
directly involved in the governmental decisions you have identified depending on
whether, based upon all the facts, it will be the subject of any of the proceedings you
have described.
Personal Finances - Regulation 18704.5 provides that"apubic official or his or
her immediate family is deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision which
has any financial effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate
fatily." Therefore, if any governmental decision you have identified has any financial
effect on the personal finances of Ms. Gregory or her husband, they are deemed to be
directly involved in the decision.
Step Five. Applicable Materiality Standard
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each
interest is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necess ary to identify
the materiality standard appropriate to each economic interest. Regulation 18i05.3 -
18705.5 sets forth the materiality standards to be applied in different situations.
Directly Involved - For economic interests in business entities directly involved
in a decision, including business entities which are a source of income to an official, the
mateiality standard is set forth in regulation 18705.1(b) which states: "[t]he financial
effects of a government decision on a business entity which is directly invtlved in
the govemmental decision is presumed to be material.,,6
UPRR and United Pacific comoration - Applying regulation 1g705.1(b), the
financial effect of any govemmental decision in which UPRR or United pacific
Corporation is directly involved is presumed to be material.
Personal Finances - A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public
official's personal finances is material if it is at least $250 in any l2-month period.
(Regulation 18705.5.)
Indirectly Involved - For economic interests in business entities indirectly
involved in a decision, including business entities which are sources of income to an
official, the materiality standards are set forth in regulation 18705.1(c). The standards
vary according to whether the business entity is a Fortune 500 company, is listed on the
New York Stock Exchange, American Stock Exchange, or NASDAQ, or according to the
size of the business entity measured by its pre-tax earnings.
6 This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any financial effect on the business entity. ('Regulation 18705.1(bxl).)
UPRR - We know that UPRR is not listed and so we focus on its earnings.
Regulation 18705.1(c)(2) applies to business entities whose most recent fiscal year pre-
tax earnings were $2.5 million or more. You have indicated that UPRR's pre-tax income
far exceeds $2.5 million. Accordingly, a governmental decision will have a material
effect upon UPRR if the decision results in an increase or decrease to UPRR's gross
revenues of $500,000 in a fiscal year' or would result in UPRR incurring or avoiding
additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $200,000 or more; or would result in an increase or decrease in the value of
UPRR's assets or liabilities of $500,000 or more.
Union Pacific Corporation - You state that Union Pacific Corporation is a Fortune
500 company. Hence, if Union Pacific Corporation is indirectly involved in a
govemmental decision, the financial effects of the decision on Union Pacific Corporation
is material if it is reasonably foreseeable the decision will result in an increase or
decrease in its gross revenues for a fiscal year of $10,000,000 or more; or will result in
Union Pacific Corporation incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or
eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $2,500,000 or more; or
will result in an increase or decrease of the value of its assets or liabilities of $ I 0,000,000
or more. (Regulation 1 8705. 1(cxl).)
Step Six. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a
goYernmental decision on Ms. Gregory's economic interests will meet the applicable
materiality standard? (Does a conflict-of-interest exist?)
'.A public official has a conflict of interest if the decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of his/her economic interests...,,
(Regulation 18700.) Once an official has determined the materiality standards applicable
to each of his or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is
"reasonably foreseeable" that the standard will be met.
A material financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably foreseeable" if
it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met as a
result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be
certain to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)
Generally, each governmental decision is analyzedindependently to determine if
there will be a foreseeable material financial effect on an official's financial interest. (/z
re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC Ops.77.) Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is
foreseeable at the time a decision is made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to
each case. (In re Tltorner, supra.) Because the Commission does not act as a finder of
fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71), the foreseeability of a
particular financial effect is a determination that must be left, in most instances, to the
informed judgment of the public official.


File No.I-06-0tI
Steps Seven & Eieht. Does thenooublic generally" exception or the .olegally
required participation, exception apply?
Even if a material financial effect on a public official's economic interest is
reasonably foreseeable, he or she still may not be disqualified if the financial effect of the
govemmental decision on the public official's "cororrri" interest is indistinguishable from
theeffect on the pubJlc generally (section 87|)3;regulations 18700(bX7) and, ty707(a))
or the official is legally required to participate (section 87l)3;r"gutatioo 1g70g). you
have provided no facts indicating that either of these exceptions applies in this instance.

QUESTION: Is Ms. Gregory, as a supervisor in the PUC's Rail Operations Safety Branch,
prohibited by the Act from performing certain duties affecting railroad companies
because her husband is employed by a railroad company that is regulated by the PUC?

CONCLUSION: As a public official employed by the PUC, Ms. Gregory may not make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use her official position to influence a
govemmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on herhusband's employer, Union Pacific Railroad Company
I Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal
written advice. (Regulation 1 8329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
2All statutoryreferences herein are to the Government
Code unless otherwise indicated. All regulatory references herein are to'I'itle 2 of the California Code of
Regulations unless otherwise indicated.
-N;ifr
("UPRR") or UPRR's parent company, Union Pacific Corporation (which owns 10002 of
UPRR's stock). Because Ms. Gregory has a community property interest in her
husband's income, she is deemed to have an economic inierest in UpRR as well as
UPRR's parent company. She must analyzeher duties to determine if they involve a
governmental decision affecting UPRR or UPRR's parent company on a case-by-case
basis.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 May 19, 2006 Ourania M. Vlahos Attorney Public Utilities Commission of the State of California 505 Van Ness Avenue San Francisco, CA 94102-3298 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-06-011 Dear Ms. Vlahos: This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Ms. Georgetta Gregory, who will be employed as a supervisor in the Rail Operations Safety Branch of the Public Utilities Commission (the "PUC"), for informal assistance' regarding the conflict-of- interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").2 QUESTION Is Ms. Gregory, as a supervisor in the PUC's Rail Operations Safety Branch, prohibited by the Act from performing certain duties affecting railroad companies because her husband is employed by a railroad company that is regulated by the PUC? CONCLUSION As a public official employed by the PUC, Ms. Gregory may not make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use her official position to influence a governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on her husband's employer, Union Pacific Railroad Company 1 Informal assistance does not confer the immunity provided by a Commission opinion or formal written advice. (Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.) 2 Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. All statutory references herein are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. All regulatory references herein are to Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations unless otherwise indicated. File No.I-06-011 Page No. 2 ("UPRR") or UPRR's parent company, Union Pacific Corporation (which owns 100% of UPRR's stock). Because Ms. Gregory has a community property interest in her husband's income, she is deemed to have an economic interest in UPRR as well as UPRR's parent company. She must analyze her duties to determine if they involve a governmental decision affecting UPRR or UPRR's parent company on a case-by-case basis. FACTS The PUC would like to employ Ms. Gregory as a supervisor ("OSS Supervisor") in the Rail Operations Safety Branch of the Operations and Safety Section. This branch is part of the PUC's Consumer Protection and Safety Division. The PUC regulates railroad companies operating in California. As OSS Supervisor, Ms. Gregory would work on matters concerning the safety of the operations of railroad companies in California. Ms. Gregory's husband is an employee of UPRR, a railroad regulated by the PUC. UPRR is one of only two long line railroad companies in California. It is a wholly owned subsidiary of Union Pacific Corporation. The OSS Supervisor oversees the work of inspectors in the Rail Operations Safety Branch who inspect railroad companies operating in California to determine whether they are complying with federal safety regulations. The Federal Railroad Administration ("FRA") promulgates regulations and imposes penalties for their violation. The FRA imposes these penalties by relying on the work of PUC inspectors. In
